elements.sent-2411-001: Conversational Recommender Systems (CRSs) aim to provide personalized recommendations through dynamically capturing user preferences in interactive conversations.
elements.sent-2411-002: Conventional CRSs often extract user preferences as hidden representations, which are criticized for their lack of interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-003: This diminishes the transparency and trustworthiness of the recommendation process.
elements.sent-2411-004: Recent works have explored combining the impressive capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) with the domain-specific knowledge of Knowledge Graphs (KGs) to generate human-understandable recommendation explanations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Despite these efforts, the integration of LLMs and KGs for CRSs remains challenging due to the modality gap between unstructured dialogues and structured KGs.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, LLMs pre-trained on large-scale corpora may not be well-suited for analyzing user preferences, which require domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper, we propose COMPASS (Compact Preference Analyzer and Summarization System), a plug-and-play framework that synergizes LLMs and KGs to unveil user preferences, enhancing the performance and explainability of existing CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-008: To address integration challenges, COMPASS employs a two-stage training approach: first, it bridges the gap between the structured KG and natural language through an innovative graph entity captioning pre-training mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-009: This enables the LLM to transform KG entities into concise natural language descriptions, allowing them to comprehend domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-010: Following, COMPASS optimizes user preference modeling via knowledge-aware instruction fine-tuning, where the LLM learns to reason and summarize user preferences from both dialogue histories and KG-augmented context.
elements.sent-2411-011: This enables COMPASS to perform knowledge-aware reasoning and generate comprehensive and interpretable user preferences that can seamlessly integrate with existing CRS models for improving recommendation performance and explainability.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of COMPASS in improving various CRS models.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/intro_fig.pdf} \caption{Conventional CRSs lack explainability in user preference modeling by extracting hidden representation, while our approach enhances transparency by generating interpretable user preference in text.} \label{fig:intro_fi}
elements.sent-2411-013: Providing personalized, context-aware recommendations that align with users' changing preferences and situational needs is a critical challenge across various domains such as e-commerce, streaming platforms, and other online services.
elements.sent-2411-014: In recent years, conversational recommender systems (CRSs) have emerged as a promising approach, harnessing the power of natural language interactions to unravel user preferences~\cite{jannach_survey_2021, GAO_advances_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: By engaging the user in interactive conversations, CRSs enable a better understanding of the user's evolving interests and guide them toward products, services, and information that best meet their immediate requirements~\cite{christakopoulou_towards_2016, li_towards_deep_2018, liu-etal-2023-conversational .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Despite the success, CRSs face unique challenges in precisely modeling user preferences from the semantically rich and dynamically evolving dialogues, due to the challenge of natural language understanding~\cite{jannach_survey_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: CRSs must capture user's intent and interest from their natural language inputs, which are often ambiguous and context-dependent~\cite{li_towards_deep_2018, zhou_improving_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Moreover, as the conversation progresses, CRSs need to continuously update and refine their understanding of user preferences in real-time~\cite{sun_conversational_recommender_2018, friedman_leveraginglargelanguagemodels_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: As the user reveals more about their preferences, CRSs must dynamically adapt their preference modeling to integrate the user's immediate interests expressed in recent interactions with their overall preferences developed over time.
elements.sent-2411-020: Conventional CRSs mainly rely on item-centric approaches~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020 , focusing on items mentioned during the conversation to model user preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Recent studies have incorporated pre-trained language models (PLMs) to enhance both natural language understanding and user preference modeling~\cite{wang_towards_2022, wang-etal-2022-recindial .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, these methods often fail to infer implicit preferences or reason about underlying motivations beyond explicit item mentions, leading to a superficial understanding of user intent.
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, they typically represent user preferences as hidden embeddings, leaving it unclear what specific preference is being considered when making a recommendation, as shown at the top of Figure~\ref{fig:intro_fi .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This ambiguity makes it challenging to verify the underlying reasons and results behind the recommendations, hindering the transparency and accountability of the recommender system.
elements.sent-2411-025: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), which significantly scale up PLMs in terms of parameter size and pre-training data, have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in both natural language processing~\cite{zhao_survey_large_language_models_2023, jiang_mistral7b_2023, dubey_llama3herdmodels_2024 and reasoning~\cite{wei_chainofthoughtprompt_2023, wang_can_small_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: These models have shown promise in generating natural language explanations, particularly in recommendation systems where explainability is critical.
elements.sent-2411-027: Recent works like X-REC~\cite{ma2024xrec and SLIM~\cite{wang_can_small_2024 have demonstrated the LLM's ability to reason over user historical behaviors and generate interpretable recommendation explanations, enhancing both explainability and performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Nevertheless, these methods only focus on the traditional recommendation settings, where user preferences are static and inferred from their historical behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-029: CRSs face unique challenges, necessitating real-time preference modeling and recommendations that adapt to users' changing preferences during ongoing conversations.
elements.sent-2411-030: Although LLMs have shown promise in CRS tasks such as evaluation~\cite{wang-etal-2023-rethinking-evaluation, zhu_how_reliable_2024 , zero-shot recommendation capabilities~\cite{he_large_language_2023 , and task planning~\cite{feng_largelanguagemodelenhanced_2023, fang_multiagentconversa_2024, li_incorporating_external_knowledge_2024 , the role of explainable user preferences in LLM-based CRSs still remains unexplored.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: While LLMs have shown promise in CRS tasks, they still face limitations in incorporating domain-specific knowledge and keeping up-to-date with item information, which are essential for accurate preference modeling and recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-032: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) have proven effective in addressing these limitations~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020, wang_towards_2022 , as they provide a rich, structured context of items and their relationships, offering domain-specific insights that enable more accurate and explainable recommendations.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Despite their demonstrated potential, integrating the structured knowledge of KGs with the reasoning and language capabilities of LLMs presents a new set of challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-034: Modality Gap (Challenge 1): There exists a significant modality gap between KGs and LLMs, which hinders the LLM's ability to understand and interpret KG information.
elements.sent-2411-035: While LLMs process sequences of tokens that represent natural language, KGs represent information in a structured, graph-based format.
elements.sent-2411-036: This difference makes it difficult for LLMs to directly interpret the entities and relationships encoded in KGs, limiting their ability to leverage domain knowledge for preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-037: Cross-Modal Reasoning (Challenge 2): Effectively reasoning over both KG information and conversation data to infer user preferences is a complex task.
elements.sent-2411-038: LLMs, despite their strong natural language processing capabilities, are not inherently designed to perform this cross-modal reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-039: Therefore, they face difficulties in analyzing and synthesizing insights from graph-structured knowledge alongside dialogue history.
elements.sent-2411-040: This limitation hinders their ability to identify relevant patterns across both sources and to perform the knowledge-aware reasoning necessary for comprehensive user preference modeling, in which both domain knowledge and user interactions are essential.
elements.sent-2411-041: To address the above challenges, we propose Compact Preference Analyzer and Summarization System (COMPASS), a novel framework that synergizes LLMs and KGs to unveil user preferences, improving both recommendation performance and the explainability of existing CRS.
elements.sent-2411-042: COMPASS directly tackles the limitations of current CRSs and the challenges of integrating LLMs with KGs through a two-stage process.
elements.sent-2411-043: First, we introduce a graph entity captioning pre-training mechanism that transforms KG structures into natural language descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-044: This allows the LLM to comprehend domain-specific information and bridge the modality gap (Challenge 1).
elements.sent-2411-045: We leverage a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to capture structural information from the KG and represent it as entity embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-046: These embeddings are then input into the LLM to produce textual descriptions of the entities along with relevant details from their neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this way, we align graph-structured knowledge with natural language, enabling the LLM to better interpret KG information.
elements.sent-2411-048: Building upon this alignment, COMPASS employs knowledge-aware instruction fine-tuning to improve the LLM's ability to reason about user preferences from dialogue histories and KG-augmented contexts.
elements.sent-2411-049: These KG-augmented contexts consist of relevant entity information and relationships extracted from the KG, providing a rich background for inference beyond the conversation history alone.
elements.sent-2411-050: Through carefully designed instructions, we enhance the LLM's capability to perform cross-modal reasoning (Challenge 2) by analyzing conversation history and cross-referencing with KG-augmented information.
elements.sent-2411-051: This instruction tuning process enhances the LLM's ability to extract explicit mentions, infer implicit interests, and reason about preferences in relation to various item attributes.
elements.sent-2411-052: Consequently, as shown in the bottom of Figure~\ref{fig:intro_fi , COMPASS generates comprehensive and interpretable user preferences in text that capture both overall preferences and current interests.}
elements.sent-2411-053: To leverage these insights, we introduce an adaptive gating mechanism that integrates summarized preferences into existing CRS models, boosting recommendation performance and explainability without requiring architectural changes.
elements.sent-2411-054: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-055: We propose COMPASS, a novel framework for enhancing user preference modeling in CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-056: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to leverage LLMs and KGs for explainable preference generation in CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-058: We develop a two-stage process that enables the LLM to perform cross-modal reasoning over KGs and conversations, generating explainable user preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-059: This approach moves beyond abstract vector representations to provide clear, human-readable user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-060: COMPASS generates user preference summaries that are compatible with existing CRS architectures without requiring modifications to the system, improving both recommendation performance and explainability.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/compass_framework.pdf} \caption{The overall framework of our COMPASS. COMPASS consists of three components: the graph encoder, the adapter, and the LLM. The adapter aligns the knowledge graph to the LLM. COMPASS follows a two-stage training paradigm - (a) Graph Entity Captioning and (b) Knowledge-aware Instruction Tuning. Once trained, COMPASS can be easily integrated with existing CRS models for (c) Preference Enhanced Recommendation.} \Description{The overall framework of our COMPASS.} \label{fig:compass}

elements.sent-2411-068: For each entity $e \in \mathcal{E}$, its description is denoted as $x_e \in \mathcal{X}$, where $x_e = [w_{1 ^{e , \ldots, w_{k ^{e ]$, and $w_{k ^{e}$ represents the $k$-th word in the entity description.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The entity set $\mathcal{E}$ encompasses candidate items $\mathcal{I}$ (e.g., movies) and non-item entities that represent item attributes (e.g., actors, genres, keywords).
elements.sent-2411-072: Explainable user preferences are crucial for enhancing the transparency and effectiveness of CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-073: Our goal is to generate clear, human-understandable textual user preference summaries that provide insights for both recommendation and explanation.
elements.sent-2411-074: Specifically, for a given dialogue history $H_t$, we define the generation of user preference summaries as: \begin{equation}\setlength\abovedisplayskip{1pt} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{1pt} \mathcal{P}_{t} = f(I_p, H_t, \mathcal{E}_{t}^{m}, \mathcal{G}),\end{equation} where $f$ represents a model that reasons over the dialogue history $H_t$, the mentioned entities $\mathcal{E _{t ^{m \subseteq \mathcal{E}$, and their associated information from the knowledge graph $\mathcal{G}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The resulting $\mathcal{P _t$ represents the textual explainable user preference summary at the $t$-th turn.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The summary $\mathcal{P _t$ is then encoded using a preference encoder $g(\cdot)$ and integrated into the base CRS model $f_{\text{crs }$, which uses it to adjust its recommendation strategy.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Depending on the model, additional inputs, such as KG information or dialogue history, may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-078: Formally, the recommendation step is represented as: \begin{equation}\setlength\abovedisplayskip{1pt} \setlength\belowdisplayskip{1pt} \mathcal{I}_t = f_{\text{crs}}(g(\mathcal{P}_t), H_t, \mathcal{G}),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{I _t$ represents the recommended items at turn $t$, and $g(\mathcal{P _t)$ denotes the encoded preference summary.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We present an overview of COMPASS, followed by detailed descriptions of each component and the training process.
elements.sent-2411-080: The primary goal of COMPASS is to synergize the reasoning capabilities of LLMs with the structured knowledge from KGs to analyze and summarize user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-081: COMPASS comprises three core components: (1) Graph encoder processes a domain-specific KG, capturing complex relationships between items to augment user preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-082: To integrate the components cohesively, we employ a two-stage training process: (1) Graph entity captioning aligns KG structures with natural language representations, creating a shared semantic space for the LLM to comprehend and reason with domain-specific knowledge effectively.
elements.sent-2411-083: Once trained, COMPASS can be integrated with existing CRS models to improve their recommendation performance and explainability by generating user preferences and incorporating them into the recommendation process with an adaptive gating mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-084: Figure~\ref{fig:compass illustrates COMPASS's architecture and training process.}
elements.sent-2411-085: In COMPASS, the KG is a crucial source of domain-specific information that provides extra context for understanding attributes and relationships of items.
elements.sent-2411-086: To efficiently convert the structured knowledge in the KG into a format understandable by the LLM for preference analysis and summarization, we utilize a Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN)~\cite{schichtkrull_modeling_2018 to capture the complex graph structure and generate entity embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-087: The R-GCN is well-suited for modeling KGs due to its ability to handle multi-relational data and capture higher-order dependencies between entities.
elements.sent-2411-088: To initialize the entity embeddings, we leverage the textual descriptions $\mathcal{X}$ associated with the entities in the KG.
elements.sent-2411-089: Specifically, for each entity $e \in \mathcal{E}$, we encode its description $x_e$ using a pre-trained language model (PLM).
elements.sent-2411-090: This provides a rich semantic foundation for the graph learning process.
elements.sent-2411-091: The R-GCN then captures both entity-level information and the overall graph structure through iterative message passing, which is particularly important for understanding the relationships between items and attributes.
elements.sent-2411-092: Formally, the representation of an entity $e$ at the $l$-th layer is calculated as follows: { \begin{equation}h_{e}^{(0)} = PLM(x_e), h_{e}^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{r\inR}\sum_{e'\in{E_{e}^{r}}}\frac{1}{Z_{e,r}}W_r^{(l)}h_{e'}^{(l)} + W_{e}^{(l)}h_{e}^{(l)} \right),\label{eq:rgcn}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{h _{e ^{l}$ is the embedding of entity $e$ at the $l$-th layer, ${\mathcal{E _{e ^{r }$ is the set of neighboring entities connected to $e$ through relation $r$, $\mathbf{W _{r ^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{W _{e ^{(l)}$ are learnable weight matrices, ${Z_{e,r }$ is a normalization factor, and $\sigma$ is an activation function.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The entity embeddings produced by the graph encoder, while rich in structural information, exist in a different representational space from the LLM's textual token representations.
elements.sent-2411-094: This makes it challenging for the LLM to effectively reason with the KG-augmented context to generate user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-095: To bridge this semantic gap and enable effective knowledge integration, we introduce an adapter module that creates a mapping between graph-structured entity embeddings and the LLM's textual domain.
elements.sent-2411-096: Specifically, the adaptation process is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{h}_{e}^{\tau} = f_P(\mathbf{h}_e),\label{eq:proj}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{h _{e ^{\tau}$ is the adapted entity embedding aligned with the LLM's semantic space, $\mathbf{h _e$ is the entity embedding generated from graph encoder, and $f_P$ is the projection function implemented as a linear layer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: The LLM is the main reasoning engine that generates user preferences by synthesizing information from dialogue histories and KG-augmented contexts.
elements.sent-2411-098: It captures both the explicit user preferences expressed in the dialogue and the implicit preferences inferred from the KG-augmented context for accurate preference analysis.
elements.sent-2411-099: Our framework is compatible with various state-of-the-art LLMs, allowing flexibility in model choice.
elements.sent-2411-100: In this paper, we use Llama3.1-8B \cite{dubey_llama3herdmodels_2024 for its natural language understanding and generation capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-101: To adapt the chosen LLM efficiently for our task, we employ Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) \cite{hu2022lora for fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-102: To obtain a Graph-to-Text adapter that effectively bridges the modality gap between the graph entity embeddings and the LLM's semantic space, inspired by the pre-training strategies employed in vision-language models~\cite{dai_instruct_blip_2023, liu_visual_ins_2023 , we introduce a graph entity captioning pretraining mechanism, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:compass a.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: This process creates a strong connection between graph-structured data and natural language by generating entity-specific captions that encompass both the entity's intrinsic information and aggregated data from its neighboring nodes, simulating a message-passing process within the graph structure.
elements.sent-2411-104: The pre-training stage enables the LLM to interpret graph entity embeddings in a semantic context, leading to a deeper understanding of the relationships and attributes encoded within the KG, which is crucial for subsequent preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-105: The caption generation process differentiates between item entities (e.g., movies) and non-item entities (e.g., genres, directors).
elements.sent-2411-106: For item entities, we employ a structured template that captures key attributes and relationships.
elements.sent-2411-107: For non-item entities, we adopt a more flexible approach that emphasizes the entity's role and its connections within the KG~\footnote{Details on caption construction and instruction templates for both item and non-item entities are provided in Appendix~\ref{app:instrution .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-108: To format the training data, an input-output pair is constructed for each entity in the knowledge graph.
elements.sent-2411-109: The input consists of the adapted entity embedding $\mathbf{h _{e ^{\tau}$ from Equation~\ref{eq:proj and a task-specific instruction prompt $I_c$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The output is the generated caption $C_e$ for the entity $e$.
elements.sent-2411-111: This format is represented as follows.
elements.sent-2411-112: This process is optimized by minimizing the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the generated captions, as expressed by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{caption} = - \sum_{e \in \mathcal{E}} \log P(C_e \mid \mathbf{h}_{e}^{\tau}, I_c),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{E}$ is the set of all entities in the KG.
elements.sent-2411-113: Through the training, the LLM learns to interpret graph entity embeddings in a semantic context, leading to a deeper understanding of the relationships and attributes encoded within the KG.
elements.sent-2411-114: This understanding is essential for improving subsequent preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-115: After the graph entity captioning pre-training, the LLM has gained a basic understanding of the KG structure and content.
elements.sent-2411-116: However, it has not yet been explicitly trained to utilize this knowledge for downstream tasks such as preference modeling and recommendation generation.
elements.sent-2411-117: To this end, we introduce a knowledge-aware instruction tuning, which aims to enable the LLM with abilities of reasoning across modalities, integrating information from both the dialogue history and the KG to infer user preferences and interests, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:compass b.}
elements.sent-2411-118: The knowledge-aware tuning process employs a carefully crafted instruction prompt~$I_p$ to guide the LLM in synthesizing and reasoning over inputs from multiple sources.
elements.sent-2411-119: Given a dialogue history~$H_t$ and the entities~$\mathcal{E _t^{m}$ mentioned within it, the process retrieves the embeddings~$\mathbf{E _t$ of these entities from Equation~\ref{eq:proj .}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: These embeddings, along with the full dialogue history, serve as inputs for the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-121: The instruction prompt~$I_p$ directs the LLM to analyze both the KG-derived entity information and the dialogue history to generate a user preference summary~$\mathcal{P _t$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: This prompt follows a coarse-to-fine structure containing four key steps: (1) reasoning, providing transparency in the model's decision-making; (2) overall preferences, offering a broad view of the user's tastes; (3) current interests, capturing recent and specific preferences to guide subsequent recommendations; and (4) recommendation, leveraging the LLM's reasoning capabilities to suggest relevant items aligned with user preferences, guiding downstream CRS models.
elements.sent-2411-123: This structured prompt ensures that the model captures both long-term preferences and immediate interests.
elements.sent-2411-124: To generate ground-truth preference summaries, we utilize an advanced LLM (e.g., ChatGPT) that performs cross-modal reasoning, integrating complete dialogue histories with structured metadata of mentioned items from the KG.
elements.sent-2411-125: The detailed process and instruction templates for this ground-truth generation are provided in Appendix \ref{app:ground-truth .}
elements.sent-2411-126: The instruction tuning process can be represented in the following format: \begin{tcolorbox [colback=cyan!10!white, colframe=cyan!60!black, title=Knowledge-aware Instruction Tuning] Input: <Mentioned Entities Embeddings $\mathbf{E _t$>, <Instruction $I_p$>, <Dialogue History $H_t$> Output: <Preference Summary $\mathcal{P _t$> \end{tcolorbox A detailed example of the preference summary can be found in Table~\ref{tab:case_study of Appendix~\ref{app:case_study .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-127: This instruction tuning process is optimized by minimizing the NLL of the generated preference summaries, as expressed by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{preference} = -\sum_{\mathcal{D}\in\mathcal{C}}\sum_{H_t\in\mathcal{D}} \log P(\mathcal{P}_t \mid \mathbf{E}_t, I_p, H_t),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{C}$ represents the set of all dialogues in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-128: Through this process, COMPASS learns to synthesize information from dialogue history and KG-derived entity embeddings, enabling it to generate comprehensive and interpretable preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-129: To enhance the existing CRS models with the user preference summaries generated by COMPASS, we propose a two-step integration process: (1) transforming the natural language preference summaries into a format compatible with CRS models, and (2) incorporating these transformed preferences to enhance the base CRS model's recommendation performance via an adaptive gating mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-130: Note that COMPASS remains frozen during this process.
elements.sent-2411-131: Therefore, we explore two methods to encode these summaries into a format suitable for existing CRS architectures: 1) PLM-based representation: This method leverages a PLM to extract rich semantic representations from text.
elements.sent-2411-132: The PLM is adaptable and can be implemented as either a frozen or trainable text encoding model.
elements.sent-2411-133: In our implementation, we employ BERT~\cite{devlin_bert_2019 to encode the user preference summary as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{s}_{c}^{\text{text}} = \text{PLM}(\mathcal{P}),\end{equation} where $\bm{s _{c ^{\text{text }$ is the encoded preference from text preference $\mathcal{P}$, specifically the [CLS] token embedding.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: The contextual understanding of language models enables $\bm{s _{c ^{\text{text $ to capture comprehensive user preference summaries at both coarse-grained (i.e., overall user preferences) and fine-grained levels (i.e., current interests and specific items).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: This approach leverages the [EOS] token embedding from the COMPASS-generated summary as it encapsulates the cumulative context of the entire preference description.
elements.sent-2411-136: We then process this embedding through a lightweight two-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to encode the preference as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{s}_{c}^{\text{EOS}} = \text{MLP}(\bm{z}_{\text{EOS}}),\end{equation} where $\bm{z _{\text{EOS }$ is the [EOS] token embedding from the COMPASS-generated preference summary, and $\bm{s _{c ^{\text{EOS }$ is the resulting encoded preference.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: This approach offers computational efficiency while still capturing the essential information from the preference summary, enabling effective integration with existing CRS models.
elements.sent-2411-138: To streamline the notation, we simplify $\bm{s _{c ^{\text{text }$ and $\bm{s _{c ^{\text{EOS }$ to $\bm{s _{c}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: We integrate the encoded preferences into the existing CRS models to enhance their recommendation performance.
elements.sent-2411-140: We employ an adaptive gating mechanism to enhance the preference representation $\bm{s _{b}$ captured from the base CRS model\footnote{The preference representation $\bm{s _{b}$ is specific to each CRS model as different models employ distinct methods for building user preferences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Formally, we have: { \begin{equation}\label{eq:gating} \gamma = \sigma(W[s_{b} ; s_c]), s_{u} = \gammas_{b} + (1 - \gamma)s_c,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{W}$ are the learnable weight matrices, $\sigma$ is the sigmoid activation function, and ${\gamma}$ represents the gating probability.}
elements.sent-2411-142: This adaptive mechanism controls the influence of each representation on the final user preference representation $\bm{s _{u}$.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The recommendation score for each item is computed using dot-product similarity as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:rec} P_{rec}(i) = \text{softmax}(\bm{s}_{u} \cdot\bm{I}_{i}),\end{equation} where $\bm{I _{i}$ are the item representations\footnote{The item representations $\bm{I _{i}$ are specific to each CRS model and may vary depending on the architecture employed by the base CRS.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: Finally, we optimize the recommendation loss $\mathcal{L _{\text{rec }$ as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{rec}} = - \sum_{j=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{M} y_{ij} \log \left( \bm{P}_{\text{rec}}^{(j)}(i) \right),\label{eq:rec_loss}\end{equation} where $N$ is the number of conversations, $M$ is the number of items, and $y_{ij}$ is the ground truth label indicating whether item $i$ is relevant to conversation $j$.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: We conduct our experiments on two widely used English CRS datasets: ReDial~\cite{li_towards_deep_2018 and INSPIRED~\cite{hayati-etal-inspired-2020 datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: The ReDial dataset, focused on movie recommendations, contains 11,348 conversations and is constructed through crowdsourcing on Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT).
elements.sent-2411-147: The INSPIRED dataset contains 999 conversations, also about movie recommendations, but additionally provides recommendation strategies based on social science theories.
elements.sent-2411-148: We constructed the knowledge graph by scraping data from IMDB\footnote{https://www.imdb.com/ , using movie titles and release years as key search terms.}

elements.sent-2411-209: In this paper, we introduce COMPASS, a novel framework that synergizes KGs and LLMs to unveil user preferences in Conversational Recommender Systems (CRSs).
elements.sent-2411-210: To address the modality gap between structured knowledge and natural language, we propose a graph entity captioning mechanism that transforms KG structures into LLM-compatible representations.
elements.sent-2411-211: Through knowledge-aware instruction tuning, COMPASS becomes proficient in performing cross-modal reasoning, generating comprehensive and interpretable user preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-212: Our proposed framework has been extensively evaluated as a plug-and-play enhancement for various existing CRS models across benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-213: The results demonstrate COMPASS's effectiveness in significantly improving both the recommendation performance and the explainability of these base models.
elements.sent-2411-214: The adaptive integration mechanism of COMPASS allows for seamless enhancement of diverse CRS architectures without requiring structural modifications, showcasing its versatility and potential for widespread adoption in the field of CRSs.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}
elements.sent-2411-215: Conversational Recommender Systems (CRSs) represent an evolution in recommendation technology, designed to understand user preferences through interactive dialogue and provide personalized recommendations~\cite{jannach_survey_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: These systems face a unique challenge in modeling user preferences from typically brief and sparse conversational data.
elements.sent-2411-217: To address this, recent works incorporate external knowledge sources into CRSs to enhance preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-218: Knowledge graphs (KG) enable systems to infer implicit user interests and capture complex relationships between items~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020 , while user reviews provide additional insights into item attributes and user opinions~\cite{lu_revcore_2021, zhou_c-crs_2022, kim_latte_2023 , helping to address the challenge of limited contextual information.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Moreover, the integration of pre-trained language models (PLMs) such as DialoGPT~\cite{zhang_dialogpt_2019, wang_towards_2022, wang-etal-2022-recindial and BART~\cite{lewis-etal-2020-bart, qiu_knowledge_graphs_2024 has further improved contextual understanding and language generation capabilities in CRSs.}}
elements.sent-2411-220: More recently, the advent of powerful LLMs has introduced new opportunities for CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-221: Studies have focused on improving LLM evaluation through enhanced user simulators~\cite{wang-etal-2023-rethinking-evaluation, zhu_how_reliable_2024 , exploring zero-shot recommendation capabilities~\cite{he_large_language_2023 , and utilizing LLMs for task planning in CRSs~\cite{feng_largelanguagemodelenhanced_2023, fang_multiagentconversa_2024, li_incorporating_external_knowledge_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-222: However, one critical aspect that remains underexplored is the role of explainable user preference in CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-223: Although some conceptual designs propose using LLMs to manage user preferences~\cite{friedman_leveraginglargelanguagemodels_2023 , they lack quantitative validation.~\cite{xi_memocrs_2024 addresses user preference memories in sequential CRS but does not fully harness LLM's reasoning abilities for preference generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Our work is the first to introduce a framework that synergizes the LLM and the KG for unveiling user preferences in CRSs, enhancing both preference modeling and recommendation explainability.
elements.sent-2411-225: Explainable recommendation has emerged as a crucial areas of research, aiming to enhance user trust, satisfaction, and decision making by providing transparent rationales for recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-226: Early research focused on leveraging user-item interactions and content features to generate simple explanations~\cite{zhang_explainable_rec_2020 , using techniques like recurrent neural networks~\cite{donker_sequential_2017 , attention mechanisms~\cite{chen_neural_att_2018 , and graph neural networks~\cite{wang_kgat_2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: Recent developments in LLMs have opened new avenues for explainable recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-228: Studies~\cite{ma2024xrec, wang_can_small_2024 have demonstrated LLMs' potential in generating explanations by analyzing user's past interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-229: However, current LLM-based methods primarily operate within the constraints of static user profiles, where preferences are inferred from historical behavior.
elements.sent-2411-230: Our work introduces a novel LLM-KG framework for CRSs, enhancing both preference modeling and recommendation explainability in dynamic interaction contexts.
elements.sent-2411-231: The detailed information for each backbone is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-232: ReDial~\cite{li_towards_deep_2018 : Introduces the ReDial dataset and model for conversational recommendation, utilizing a denoising auto-encoder\cite{he_neural_coll_2017 as the recommendation module.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: KBRD~\cite{chen_towards_2019 : This method leverages an external KG to enhance the semantics of entities mentioned in the dialogue history.}
elements.sent-2411-234: KGSF~\cite{zhou_improving_2020 : This method employs a dual KG approach, integrating semantic information from both word-level and entity-level KGs.}
elements.sent-2411-235: It applies a mutual information maximization technique to align these two semantic spaces, enhancing the overall representation.
elements.sent-2411-236: BERT~\cite{devlin_bert_2019 : This method utilizes a bidirectional Transformer model~\cite{Vaswani_attention_is_all_2017 pre-trained on a large-scale corpus.}}
elements.sent-2411-237: We use the [CLS] token representation for the recommendation task.
elements.sent-2411-239: We fine-tune it on dialogue history and use the last token representation for the recommendation task.
elements.sent-2411-240: Llama3.1-8B~\cite{dubey_llama3herdmodels_2024 : This is an open-source LLM that has been trained using both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning with human feedback~\cite{ouyang_2022_traininglanguagemodelsfollow .}}
elements.sent-2411-241: For the recommendation task, we fine-tune LLama3.1-8B using LoRA on the dialogue history and use the last token representation as the user preference embedding.
elements.sent-2411-242: BARCOR~\cite{wang2022barcorunifiedframeworkconversational : This model introduces a unified framework that integrates BART with a knowledge graph, enabling both recommendation and response generation within a single model.}
elements.sent-2411-243: PECRS~\cite{ravaut-etal-2024-parameter : This model integrates recommendation and response generation into a single training phase using LoRA~\cite{hu2022lora with a pre-trained language model, eliminating the need for separate modules or external knowledge graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-244: This study introduces a novel approach to enhance CRS by generating user preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-245: As there are no existing enhancers specifically designed for this task, we use Llama3.1-8B\footnote{https://ollama.com/ and \text{GPT-4o}\footnote{gpt-4o-2024-05-13 as baselines for comparison, both of which generate user preference summaries without access to KG information.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: To clarify, Llama3.1-8B is used in two different capacities in this work: first, as a backbone CRS model adapted through training for the recommendation task, and second, as Llama-Summary for generating user preference summaries without KG access.
elements.sent-2411-247: We enhance CRS models performance by incorporating our COMPASS framework as described in Section~\ref{sec:preference_enhanced_recommendation .}
elements.sent-2411-248: The integration method is consistent across KBRD, KGSF, and BERT, utilizing the adaptive gating mechanism to combine COMPASS-generated preference summaries with the original model representations.
elements.sent-2411-249: For preference-enhanced Llama3, we employ a different integration strategy.

elements.sent-2411-256: The caption templates for various entity types in the KG (such as genres, actors, directors, companies, and keywords) are presented in Table~\ref{tab:caption_templates .}
elements.sent-2411-257: These templates guide the translation of graph structures into natural language descriptions, emphasizing each entity's relationships within the domain.
elements.sent-2411-258: The templates enable COMPASS to generate contextual descriptions that preserve graph structure while presenting information in a format compatible with the LLM, facilitating effective cross-modal analysis.
elements.sent-2411-259: The model performed cross-modal reasoning by integrating the complete dialogue history with structured metadata of items mentioned in the conversation, derived from the KG.
elements.sent-2411-260: We developed a structured instruction template, presented in Table~\ref{tab:gpt4 , to guide this analysis process.}
elements.sent-2411-261: This template outlines a step-by-step approach for discerning user preferences through conversation analysis, KG cross-referencing, and insight synthesis, resulting in a comprehensive user preference summary.
elements.sent-2411-262: Following the method proposed by~\cite{wang-etal-2023-chatgpt , we adopt an LLM-based evaluation approach where GPT-4o-mini serves as an automated scorer to evaluate the reasoning proficiency (RP) and fact consistency (FC) of the user preference summaries generated.}
elements.sent-2411-263: The evaluation of RP is centered on three main criteria: (1) Logical Coherence, which assesses the internal consistency of the reasoning process; (2) Accuracy of Inferences, which measures how precisely deductions are made from the dialogue and KG data; and (3) Relevance of Recommendations, which evaluates whether the suggested items are appropriate given the user's expressed interests.
elements.sent-2411-264: FC evaluation similarly considers three aspects: (1) Dialogue Alignment, which measures fidelity to user-expressed preferences; (2) KG Consistency, assessing the accuracy of preference keywords relative to the KG content; and (3) Claim Validity, checking for unsupported assertions while allowing for reasonable expansions.
elements.sent-2411-265: The evaluation uses the dialogue history, KG data related to the mentioned entities in the dialogue, and the generated preference summary as input.
elements.sent-2411-266: GPT-4o-mini then analyzes these inputs and generates scores for RP and FC on a scale of 0-100.
elements.sent-2411-267: The average scores are calculated for all test samples.
elements.sent-2411-268: To complement the quantitative metrics with user-oriented insights, a user-centric evaluation is performed using GPT-4o-mini as a simulated user, following the approach outlined by~\cite{ji2023exploringimpactinstructiondata, gao2024llmbasednlgevaluationcurrent .}
elements.sent-2411-269: We evaluate the Explainability and User Preference Alignment of the generated preference summaries and recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-270: Explainability refers to how clearly the system justifies its summaries and recommendations in a way that is easily understandable from the user’s perspective.
elements.sent-2411-271: User Preference Alignment assesses how accurately the system captures and responds to the preferences expressed by the user in the conversation.
elements.sent-2411-272: The evaluation uses the dialogue history and preference summaries from different models as inputs.
elements.sent-2411-273: GPT-4o-mini ranks these summaries according to the EX and UPA criteria, providing justifications for each ranking.

elements.sent-2411-274: Our COMPASS framework demonstrates versatility in generating user preference summaries, offering both comprehensive textual descriptions and compact EOS token embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-275: To integrate these outputs into existing CRS models, we evaluated two encoding methods: a lightweight encoder and a PLM-based text encoder.
elements.sent-2411-276: Table \ref{tab:ab_encoder presents results for KBRD, KGSF, and BERT models on the ReDial dataset, which shows the effectiveness of both approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-277: The PLM-based text encoder (108M parameters) excels in processing COMPASS's textual summaries, achieving superior performance across all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-278: Conversely, the lightweight encoder (16.8M parameters, \(\sim \)15.5\% of PLM-based) efficiently processes COMPASS's EOS token embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-279: Despite its simplicity, it significantly improves on the baseline performance, increasing KBRD's HR@10 and HR@50 by 24.5\% and 12.8\% respectively.
elements.sent-2411-280: This demonstrates COMPASS's capability to distill user preferences into compact yet informative representations.
elements.sent-2411-281: Both methods enhance performance across different base models, highlighting COMPASS's adaptability in generating preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-282: This versatility enables flexible deployment across various computational environments, from resource-constrained systems to high-performance platforms, thereby accommodating a wide range of application scenarios.

elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{Instruction Template for Ground Truth Generation} \label{tab:gpt4} \begin{tabular}{p{0.9\textwidth}} Instruction Template As a personalized movie recommender, your task is to analyze the user's conversation history and relevant movie data from the knowledge graph to discern a balanced mix of specific movie titles and broader thematic and stylistic movie preferences. Review Conversation History: Extract mentions of movies and any expressed preferences in genres, keywords, actors, directors, or production companies from the dialogue. 2. Cross-Reference with Knowledge Graph: Correlate the extracted movie preferences from the conversation with the knowledge graph data, looking for patterns in genres, keywords, actors, directorial styles and etc. 3. Synthesize Insights: Combine the conversation and knowledge graph analysis to create a profile of the user's movie preferences, highlighting both specific movies and general thematic interests. \# Input Example \#\# Conversation History \$User:\{\} \$Recommender: \{\} \#\# Knowledge Graph (1). \$\{movie name\}(\$year); Genres: \$\{Genre\}; Keywords: \$\{Keywords\}; Starring: \$\{Starring\}; Director: \$\{Director\}; Company: \$\{Company\}; Plot: \$\{Plot\} ... \# Expected Output JSON Format Response \{ "reasoning": "A concise explanation reflecting the Analysis Steps, detailing how specific movie and broader thematic and stylistic preferences were identified and integrated.", "overall preferences": "A short list of keywords summarizing the user's general thematic and stylistic movie preferences, including genres, notable keywords, preferred actors, directors, narrative styles, etc.", "current interest": "A concise list of keywords reflecting the user's most recent and pertinent thematic and stylistic interests, guiding the next recommendations." \} Conversation History: [Conversation history is inserted here] Knowledge Graph: [Mentioned Items Meta Information from Knowledge Graph is inserted here] \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-295: Conversational Recommender Systems (CRSs) aim to provide personalized recommendations by capturing user preferences through interactive dialogues.
elements.sent-2411-296: Explainability in CRSs is crucial as it enables users to understand the reasoning behind recommendations, increasing system transparency and trustworthiness.
elements.sent-2411-297: However, current CRSs often leverage knowledge graphs (KGs) or language models to extract and represent user preferences as latent vectors, which limits their explainability.
elements.sent-2411-298: Large language models (LLMs) offer powerful reasoning capabilities that can bridge this gap by generating human-understandable preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-299: However, effectively reasoning over user preferences in CRSs remains challenging as LLMs pre-trained on large-scale corpora may not be well-suited for analyzing user preferences, which requires domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-300: While KGs provide rich domain knowledge, integrating them with LLMs encounters a significant modality gap between structured KG information and unstructured conversations.
elements.sent-2411-301: In this paper, we propose COMPASS (Compact Preference Analyzer and Summarization System), a plug-and-play framework that synergizes LLMs and KGs to reason over user preferences, enhancing the performance and explainability of existing CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-302: COMPASS employs a two-stage training approach: first, it bridges the gap between the structured KG and natural language through novel graph entity captioning pre-training.
elements.sent-2411-303: This enables the LLM to transform KG entities into natural language descriptions, allowing it to comprehend domain-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-304: Next, COMPASS optimizes user preference reasoning via knowledge-aware instruction fine-tuning, where the LLM learns to reason and summarize user preferences from dialogue histories and KG-augmented context.
elements.sent-2411-305: This enables COMPASS to perform knowledge-aware reasoning and generate interpretable user preferences that can seamlessly integrate with existing CRS models for improving recommendation performance and explainability.
elements.sent-2411-306: By engaging the user in interactive conversations, CRSs enable a better understanding of the user's evolving interests and guide them toward products, services, and information that best meet their immediate requirements~\cite{christakopoulou_towards_2016, li_towards_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-307: Despite the success, CRSs face unique challenges in precisely modeling user preferences from semantically rich and dynamically evolving dialogue.
elements.sent-2411-308: They must interpret often ambiguous and context-dependent natural language inputs~\cite{li_towards_2018, zhou_improving_2020 , while continuously updating and refining their understanding of user preferences in real-time~\cite{sun_conversational_2018 , integrating immediate interests with overall preferences developed over time.}}
elements.sent-2411-309: Conventional CRSs mainly depends on item-centric approaches~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020 , focusing on explicitly mentioned items in the conversation to model user preference.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Recent studies incorporating pre-trained language models (PLMs) to enhance both natural language understanding and user preference modeling~\cite{wang_towards_2022, wang-etal-2022-recindial .}
elements.sent-2411-311: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs), have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in both language understanding~\cite{zhao2025surveylargelanguagemodels,grattafiori2024llama3herdmodels and complex reasoning~\cite{wei_chainofthoughtprompt_2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-312: These capabilities make LLMs promising for explainable recommendations, and recent works~\cite{ma2024xrec, wang_can_2024,li_g-refer_2025 have demonstrated the LLM's ability to reason over user historical behaviors and generate interpretable recommendation explanations.}
elements.sent-2411-313: Nevertheless, these methods only focus on the traditional recommendation settings, where user preferences are static.
elements.sent-2411-314: In contrast, CRSs require more sophisticated reasoning to model user preferences that evolve dynamically throughout conversation.
elements.sent-2411-315: While recent work has explored LLMs for various CRS tasks, including evaluation~\cite{wang-etal-2023-rethinking-evaluation, zhu_how_reliable_2024 , zero-shot recommendation~\cite{he_large_language_2023 , and task planning~\cite{feng_largelanguagemodelenhanced_2023, fang_multiagentconversa_2024 , the role of explainable user preferences in LLM-based CRSs remains unexplored.}}}
elements.sent-2411-316: Enabling LLMs to reason over user preferences requires more than their general language capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-317: Effective preference reasoning requires domain-specific knowledge about item attributes, relationships, and up-to-date information that LLMs inherently lack.
elements.sent-2411-318: Knowledge graphs (KGs) provide this essential structured knowledge through rich semantic relationship and have proven effective in CRSs~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020, wang_towards_2022, qiu_kerl_2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-319: Despite their demonstrated potential, integrating KGs with LLMs for preference reasoning presents fundamental challenges that existing approaches have not addressed:.
elements.sent-2411-320: Modality Gap (Challenge 1): There exists a significant modality gap between KGs and LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-321: While LLMs process sequences of tokens representing natural language, KGs represent information in structured, graph-based formats with explicit entity relationships.
elements.sent-2411-322: This representational mismatch limits LLMs's ability to directly interpret and reason with the domain knowledge encoded in KGs.
elements.sent-2411-323: Cross Modal Reasoning (Challenge 2): Effectively reasoning over both the KG and conversation to infer user preferences is a complex task.
elements.sent-2411-324: This limitation hinders their ability to identify relevant patterns across both sources and to perform the knowledge-aware reasoning necessary for comprehensive user preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-325: To address the above challenges, we propose Compact Preference Analyzer and Summarization System (COMPASS), a novel framework that augments the LLM with the KG to enable explainable preference reasoning, improving both recommendation performance and the explainability of existing CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-326: COMPASS introduces a two-stage training paradigm that fundamentally transforms how LLMs process and reason with structured knowledge for preference analysis.
elements.sent-2411-327: First, we introduce graph entity captioning pre-training that transforms KG structures into natural language descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-328: We employ a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to encode structural information from the KG as entity embeddings, which are then projected into the LLM's semantic space via an adapter module.
elements.sent-2411-329: The LLM learns to decode these projected embeddings into textual descriptions of entities and their relationships, effectively translating graph-structured knowledge into a format compatible with its reasoning mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-330: Building upon this alignment, COMPASS employs knowledge-aware instruction fine-tuning to enhance the LLM's ability to reason about user preferences from dialogue histories and KG-augmented contexts.
elements.sent-2411-331: To leverage these insights, we introduce an adaptive gating mechanism that integrates summarized preferences into existing CRSs, boosting recommendation performance and explainability without requiring architectural changes.
elements.sent-2411-332: Knowledge graphs enable systems to infer implicit user interests and capture item relationships~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020 , Conversational Recommender System.}
elements.sent-2411-333: Conversational recommender systems (CRSs) understand user preferences through interactive dialogue to provide personalized recommendations~\cite{jannach_survey_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-334: Knowledge graphs enable systems to infer implicit user interests and capture item relationships~\cite{chen_towards_2019, zhou_improving_2020 ,, while user reviews provide additional insights into item attributes and user opinions~\cite{lu_revcore_2021, zhou_c-crs_2022 , helping to address the challenge of limited contextual information.}}
elements.sent-2411-335: Moreover, pre-trained language models like DialoGPT~\cite{wang_towards_2022, wang-etal-2022-recindial and BART~\cite{wang2022barcorunifiedframeworkconversational,qiu_kerl_2025 has further improved contextual understanding and language generation capabilities in CRSs.}}
elements.sent-2411-336: Studies have focused on improving LLM evaluation through enhanced user simulators~\cite{wang-etal-2023-rethinking-evaluation, zhu_how_reliable_2024 , exploring zero-shot recommendation capabilities~\cite{he_large_language_2023 , using graph-based retrieval augmentation for in-context learning~\cite{qiu_gcrs_2025 , and utilizing LLMs for task planning~\cite{feng_largelanguagemodelenhanced_2023, fang_multiagentconversa_2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-337: Although some conceptual designs propose using LLMs to manage user preferences~\cite{friedman_leveraginglargelanguagemodels_2023 , they lack quantitative validation.}
elements.sent-2411-338: MemoCRS~\cite{xi_memocrs_2024 addresses user preference memories in sequential CRS but does not fully harness LLM's reasoning abilities for preference generation.}
elements.sent-2411-339: Our work introduces the first framework that augments LLMs with KG for reasoning over user preferences in CRSs, enhancing both preference modeling and recommendation explainability.
elements.sent-2411-340: Our work introduces the first framework that augments LLMs with KG for reasoning over user preferences in CRSs, enhancing both preference modeling and recommendation explainability.. Explainable Recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-341: Explainable recommendation aims to enhance user trust and decision-making by providing transparent rationales for recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-342: Early research leveraged user-item interactions and content features to generate simple explanations~\cite{zhang_explainable_rec_2020 , using techniques like recurrent neural networks~\cite{donker_sequential_2017 , attention mechanisms~\cite{chen_neural_att_2018 , and graph neural networks~\cite{wang_kgat_2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-343: Recent LLMs have shown potential in generating explanations by analyzing user interactions~\cite{ma2024xrec, wang_can_2024 , but primarily operate within static user profiles where preferences are inferred from historical behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-344: Our work introduces a novel KG-augmented LLM framework for CRSs, enhancing both preference modeling and recommendation explainability in dynamic interaction contexts.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/compass_framework.pdf} \caption{The overall framework of our COMPASS. COMPASS consists of three components: the graph encoder, the adapter, and the LLM. The adapter aligns the knowledge graph to the LLM. COMPASS follows a two-stage training paradigm - (a) Graph Entity Captioning and (b) Knowledge-aware Instruction Tuning. Once trained, COMPASS can be easily integrated with existing CRS models for (c) Preference Enhanced Recommendation.} \label{fig:compass}
elements.sent-2411-345: The CRS estimates the user's preferences based on $H_t$, then recommend $K$ items from $\mathcal{I}$, which are used to generate the next utterance $u_{t+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-346: To efficiently convert the structured knowledge in the KG into a format understandable by the LLM for preference reasoning and summarization, we utilize a Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN)~\cite{schichtkrull_modeling_2018 to capture the complex graph structure and generate entity embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Specifically, for each entity $e \in \mathcal{E}$, we encode its description $x_{e}$ using a PLM.
elements.sent-2411-348: Formally, the representation of an entity $e$ at the $l$-th layer is calculated as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{h}_{e}^{(0)}= \text{PLM}(x_{e}), \label{eq:rgcn_init}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathbf{h}_{e}^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{r\in\mathcal{R}}\sum_{e'\in{\mathcal{E}_{e}^{r}}}\frac{1}{Z_{e,r}}\mathbf{W}_{r}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_{e'}^{(l)} + \mathbf{W}_{e}^{(l)}\mathbf{h}_{e}^{(l)} \right), \label{eq:rgcn}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{h _{e ^{l}$ is the embedding of entity $e$ at the $l$-th layer, ${\mathcal{E _{e ^{r }$ is the set of neighboring entities connected to $e$ through relation $r$, $\mathbf{W _{r ^{(l)}$ and $\mathbf{W _{e ^{(l)}$ are learnable weight matrices, ${Z_{e,r }$ is a normalization factor, and $\sigma$ is an activation function.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-349: The entity embeddings from the graph encoder exist in a different representational space from the LLM's textual token representations.
elements.sent-2411-350: Specifically, the adaptation process is defined as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{h}_{e}^{\tau}= f_{P}(\mathbf{h}_{e}),\label{eq:proj}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{h _{e ^{\tau}$ is the adapted entity embedding aligned with the LLM's semantic space, $\mathbf{h _{e}$ is the entity embedding generated from graph encoder, and $f_{P}$ is the projection function implemented as a linear layer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-351: It captures both the explicit user preferences expressed in the dialogue and the implicit preferences inferred from the KG-augmented context for accurate preference reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-352: To obtain a Graph-to-Text adapter that bridges the modality gap between the graph entity embeddings and the LLM's semantic space, inspired by the pre-training strategies employed in vision-language models~\cite{dai_instruct_blip_2023, liu_visual_ins_2023 , we introduce a graph entity captioning pre-training mechanism, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:compass a.}}
elements.sent-2411-353: This process creates a strong connection between graph-structured data and natural language by generating entity-specific captions that combine intrinsic entity information with neighboring node data, simulating message-passing within the graph structure.
elements.sent-2411-354: The pre-training stage enables the LLM to interpret graph entity embeddings in a semantic context, facilitating deeper understanding of KG relationships and attributes for preference modeling.
elements.sent-2411-355: For non-item entities, we adopt a more flexible approach that emphasizes the entity's role and its connections within the KG.
elements.sent-2411-356: The input consists of the adapted entity embedding $\mathbf{h _{e ^{\tau}$ from Equation~\ref{eq:proj and a task-specific instruction prompt $I _{c}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-357: The output is the generated caption $C_{e}$ for the entity $e$.
elements.sent-2411-358: This process is optimized by minimizing the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the generated captions, as expressed by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{caption}= - \sum_{e \in \mathcal{E}}\log P(C_{e}\mid \mathbf{h}_{e} ^{\tau}, I_{c}),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{E}$ is the set of all entities in the KG.
elements.sent-2411-359: To this end, we introduce knowledge-aware instruction tuning to enable the LLM to reason across modalities, integrating dialogue history and KG information to infer user preferences and interests, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:compass b.}
elements.sent-2411-360: The knowledge-aware tuning process employs a carefully crafted instruction prompt~$I _{p}$ to guide the LLM in synthesizing and reasoning over inputs from multiple sources.
elements.sent-2411-361: Given a dialogue history~$H_{t}$ and the entities~$\mathcal{E _{t ^{m}$ mentioned within it, the process retrieves the embeddings~$\mathbf{E _{t}$ of these entities from Equation~\ref{eq:proj .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-362: The instruction prompt~$I_{p}$ directs the LLM to analyze both the KG-derived entity information and the dialogue history to generate a user preference summary~$\mathcal{P _{t}$.}
elements.sent-2411-363: The instruction tuning process can be represented in the following format: \begin{tcolorbox [colback=cyan!10!white, colframe=cyan!60!black, fontupper= , fonttitle= , boxsep=2pt, title=Knowledge-aware Instruction Tuning] Input: \textless Mentioned Entities Embeddings $\mathbf{E _{t}$\textgreater, \textless Instruction $I_{p}$\textgreater, \textless Dialogue History $H_{t}$\textgreater Output: \textless Preference Summary $\mathcal{P _{t}$\textgreater \end{tcolorbox A detailed example of the preference summary can be found in Table~\ref{tab:case_study .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-364: This instruction tuning process is optimized by minimizing the NLL of the generated preference summaries, as expressed by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{preference}= -\sum_{\mathcal{D}\in\mathcal{C}}\sum_{H_t\in\mathcal{D}} \log P(\mathcal{P}_{t}\mid \mathbf{E}_{t}, I_{p}, H_{t}),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{C}$ represents the set of all dialogues in the training data.
elements.sent-2411-365: We employ a PLM-based encoder to transform these summaries into a format suitable for existing CRS architectures.
elements.sent-2411-366: The encoder is adaptable and can be implemented as either a frozen or trainable text encoding model.
elements.sent-2411-367: We employ BERT~\cite{devlin_bert_2019 to encode the user preference summary as follows: \begin{equation}\bm{s}_{c}= \text{PLM}(\mathcal{P}),\end{equation} where $\bm{s _{c}$ is the encoded preference from text preference $\mathcal{P}$, specifically the [CLS] token embedding.}}
elements.sent-2411-368: The contextual understanding of language models enables $\bm{s _{c}$ to capture comprehensive user preference summaries at both coarse-grained (i.e., overall user preferences) and fine-grained levels (i.e., current interests and specific items).}
elements.sent-2411-369: We integrate the encoded preferences into existing CRS models to enhance their recommendation performance.
elements.sent-2411-370: We employ an adaptive gating mechanism to enhance the preference representation $\bm{s _{b}$ captured from the base CRS model \footnote{The preference representation $\bm{s _{b}$ is specific to each CRS model as different models employ distinct methods for building user preferences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-371: Note that the preference representation $\bm{s _{b}$ is specific to each CRS model as different models employ distinct methods for building user preferences.}

elements.sent-2411-380: Since no existing baselines specifically generate user preference summaries, we use Llama3.1-8B and GPT-4o as baselines for comparison, both without KG access.

elements.sent-2411-383: To evaluate user preference summaries, we employ three complementary metrics: (1) Lexical Similarity: ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and ROUGE-Sum; (2) Semantic Understanding: Following~\cite{wang-etal-2023-chatgpt , we use GPT-4o-mini to assess Reasoning Proficiency (RP) and Factual Consistency (FC).}
elements.sent-2411-384: RP assesses logical coherence, inference accuracy, and recommendation relevance, while FC evaluates dialogue alignment, knowledge graph consistency, and statement accuracy.

elements.sent-2411-419: In this paper, we introduce COMPASS, a novel framework that augments LLMs with KGs to reason over user preferences in CRSs.
elements.sent-2411-420: To address the modality gap between structured knowledge and natural language, we propose a graph entity captioning that transforms KG structures into LLM-compatible representations.
elements.sent-2411-421: Through knowledge-aware instruction tuning, COMPASS becomes proficient in performing cross-modal reasoning, generating interpretable user preference summaries.
elements.sent-2411-422: COMPASS has been extensively evaluated as a plug-and-play enhancement for various CRSs across benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-423: The results demonstrate its effectiveness in significantly improving both recommendation performance and explainability of these base models.
elements.sent-2411-424: The adaptive integration mechanism of COMPASS allows for seamless enhancement of diverse CRS architectures without structural modifications, showcasing its versatility and potential for widespread adoption in the field of CRSs.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
